query:
UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Use the 4-to-1 rule: For every 4 feet of height, the base should be 1 foot from the wall. Example: a 16-foot ladder should have its base 4 feet from the wall.
• Extension ladders must extend at least 3 feet above the landing surface. Secure the top and bottom — never rely on someone holding it.
• Always maintain three points of contact: two hands and one foot, or two feet and one hand. Never carry tools while climbing — use a tool belt or hoist line.
• Inspect ladders before each use: check for bent rails, cracked rungs, loose hardware, and missing feet. Damaged ladders must be tagged out and removed from the job site immediately.
• Never use a metal ladder near electrical work. Use fiberglass ladders around any electrical hazard.
• Set up on firm, level ground. If the ground is soft, use a mud sill (plywood base). Never place a ladder on boxes, barrels, or unstable surfaces.
• Real-world example: A worker stepped on a rung that had a hairline crack — it snapped under his weight at 8 feet. He broke his wrist and missed 6 weeks of work. A 10-second inspection would have caught it.

Discussion Questions:
1. Where are the ladders on this job site, and are they all in good condition?
2. Has anyone seen a ladder set up incorrectly this week?
3. What''s the proper angle for the ladder we''re using today?

Sign-off: Every crew member must confirm they understand ladder setup and inspection before using one today.' WHERE `id` = 2;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Only competent persons may erect, modify, or dismantle scaffolding. OSHA defines a competent person as someone who can identify hazards and has authority to correct them (29 CFR 1926.451).
• Scaffolds must support 4x the maximum intended load. Never overload with materials — keep the platform clear of debris and only stage what you need.
• Guardrails are required on all open sides at 38-45 inches high, with a midrail and toeboard. If guardrails are removed for material loading, fall protection must be used.
• Inspect scaffolding daily before use: check base plates, cross braces, planking, and connections. After any storm, high wind, or seismic event, a competent person must re-inspect before work resumes.
• Never use makeshift scaffolding — no stacked pallets, sawhorses with planks, or improvised platforms. If the right scaffold isn''t available, stop work and request it.
• Planking must extend at least 6 inches beyond the support but no more than 12 inches. Gaps between planks should not exceed 1 inch.
• Real-world example: A crew was using a scaffold with a missing cross brace. When a worker stepped to the edge, the frame shifted and the platform collapsed. Two workers were injured. The missing brace was noticed that morning but not reported.

Discussion Questions:
1. Who is the competent person for scaffold inspection on this job?
2. Are all guardrails and toeboards in place on our current scaffolding?
3. When was the last time our scaffolding was formally inspected?

Sign-off: Report any scaffold deficiency immediately — do not use it until it''s fixed.' WHERE `id` = 3;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Assume all power lines are energized. OSHA requires a minimum 10-foot clearance from overhead power lines for workers and equipment (29 CFR 1926.416).
• Before any work near electrical panels, circuits, or wiring, verify the power is off using a voltage tester — never trust labels or breaker positions alone.
• Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters (GFCIs) are required on all 120-volt, single-phase, 15- and 20-amp outlets on construction sites. Test GFCIs monthly and before each use.
• Water and electricity don''t mix. Never use electrical tools in wet conditions unless they''re rated for it. Keep cords out of standing water and puddles.
• Extension cords must be rated for the tool''s amperage. Inspect cords for cuts, exposed wires, and damaged plugs. Never use tape to repair a cord — replace it.
• If someone is being electrocuted, do NOT touch them. Disconnect the power source first, then call 911. Use a non-conductive object (dry wood, rubber) to separate them from the source if power can''t be cut.
• Real-world example: A framer was cutting near an unmarked live wire in a wall. His saw blade hit the wire and the shock threw him off the ladder. Always verify what''s behind the wall before cutting.

Discussion Questions:
1. Where are the electrical panels and live circuits on this job site?
2. Are all our GFCIs tested and working?
3. What would you do if you saw a coworker getting shocked?

Sign-off: Electricity kills silently. Always test before you touch.' WHERE `id` = 4;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) prevents unexpected startup of machinery or release of stored energy during maintenance. OSHA standard 29 CFR 1910.147 applies.
• The six steps of LOTO: (1) Notify all affected employees, (2) Shut down the equipment using normal procedures, (3) Isolate all energy sources, (4) Apply your personal lock and tag, (5) Release any stored energy (hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, gravitational), (6) Verify the equipment is de-energized by attempting to start it.
• Every worker performing maintenance must apply their OWN lock. Never share locks or let someone else lock out for you. Each person''s lock = each person''s life.
• Stored energy is often forgotten. Hydraulic systems hold pressure, springs store mechanical energy, capacitors hold electrical charge, and elevated components have gravitational energy. All must be released or blocked.
• Tags alone are NOT sufficient — they must be used WITH locks. A tag is a warning; a lock is a physical barrier.
• Never remove someone else''s lock. If a worker leaves without removing their lock, follow your company''s specific procedure for lock removal — it requires supervisor authorization and verification.
• Real-world example: A maintenance worker was cleaning a conveyor belt. Another worker didn''t see the tag and restarted the machine. The maintenance worker lost three fingers. A lock would have prevented the restart.

Discussion Questions:
1. What equipment on this job requires LOTO procedures?
2. Does everyone have their own personal lock and tag?
3. Walk through the LOTO steps for one piece of equipment we use.

Sign-off: If it can move, store energy, or start up — lock it out.' WHERE `id` = 5;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
• OSHA requires protective systems for all trenches 5 feet or deeper (29 CFR 1926.652). Options include sloping, shoring, or trench boxes. No exceptions.
• A competent person must inspect the trench daily before work begins and after any rain, vibration, or change in conditions. They must classify the soil type (A, B, or C) to determine the required protection.
• Never enter a trench without checking for atmospheric hazards — oxygen deficiency, toxic gases (hydrogen sulfide), and methane can accumulate in excavations. Use a 4-gas monitor.
• Keep spoil piles at least 2 feet from the trench edge. Heavy equipment should be kept back from the edge — vibration can cause cave-ins.
• Provide a means of egress (ladder, ramp, or stairs) within 25 feet of every worker in the trench. If you can''t get out fast, you shouldn''t be in there.
• One cubic yard of soil weighs approximately 3,000 pounds — that''s the weight of a car. A trench collapse can crush and suffocate a worker in seconds. There is almost no time to react.
• Real-world example: In Utah, a utility worker was buried in a 6-foot trench that had no shoring. The sandy soil collapsed when a truck drove nearby. It took rescue crews 45 minutes to reach him — he did not survive. The trench box was on site but hadn''t been placed.

Discussion Questions:
1. What type of soil are we working in on this job?
2. Where are the egress points in our current excavation?
3. Is our competent person doing daily inspections before anyone enters?

Sign-off: A trench is a grave waiting to happen. Never skip the protection.' WHERE `id` = 6;
